title: JTBD Analysis: Continuing Care Retirement Communities (NAICS 623311)

sections.title: Phase 1 — Industry Landscape
sections.content:
### 1.1 CFJ, Output Types, Offering Level

| NAICS | CFJ | Output Types | Offering Level |
|---|---|---|---|
| 623311 | Provide safe, integrated residential accommodation and healthcare services across a continuum of care levels to older adults, enabling aging in place with progressively intensive clinical support as functional needs evolve | Independent Living (IL), Assisted Living (AL), Memory Care (MC), Skilled Nursing (SNF), Post-Acute Rehab (PAR) | L7 — Ecosystem |

**Note:** Service NAICS. No Product BOM. Steps 1.4 (BOM) and 1.5 (Edges) are skipped per routing rules.

---

### 1.2 Segments (by circumstance)

| # | Segment | Circumstance | Why distinct |
|---|---|---|---|
| S1 | Memory Care Unit | Residents with dementia/cognitive impairment; 60% at elopement risk; ~1–2% elope annually; 32–40% of elopements fatal within 24 h [[Tangram]](https://tangramins.com/news-insights/shocking-elopement-statistics-how-to-keep-wandering-residents-safe/) | Alternatives differ: wander management (RFID tags) dominates; exits must be secured against resident-initiated actuation while remaining fire-code compliant |
| S2 | Independent + Assisted Living | Functionally capable residents; no resident-initiated elopement risk; standard fire exit compliance | Simpler alternatives hired: basic mechanical alarms, wired panels; lower urgency |
| S3 | Skilled Nursing Facility (SNF) | 24-h clinical care; highest CMS regulatory scrutiny; annual Life Safety survey; Immediate Jeopardy penalties >$10,000/day for elopement gaps [[CMS]](https://www.cms.gov/medicare/health-safety-standards/certification-compliance/life-safety-code-health-care-facilities-code-requirements) | Compliance documentation and audit-trail quality are the primary buying criteria |
| S4 | Post-Acute Rehabilitation | Short-stay, high resident throughput; fire safety primary; low elopement risk | Simpler exit monitoring acceptable; false-alarm tolerance higher than MC |
| S5 | Multi-Facility Portfolio Operator | Manages many campuses; procurement is portfolio-level; needs centralized dashboard across properties | Different alternatives hired: enterprise wander-management platforms vs. per-door retrofit devices |

**Primary Türwächter segments:** S1 (highest urgency) and S3 (compliance documentation imperative).

---

### 1.3 Process VN

→ Full VN in `VN_continuing_care_retirement.md`. Türwächter-relevant nodes:

| Node | Level | Job family | Türwächter relevance |
|---|---|---|---|
| L6H-SAF — Facility Safety & Emergency Management | L6 (horizontal) | Protect residents, staff, and assets from safety incidents at all times | **Primary anchor** — Emergency Exit Door Alarm Guards sits at L5 under this node |
| L6d — Memory Care Services | L6 (core) | Provide secured residential care for residents with dementia and cognitive impairment | **Secondary context** — Wander Prevention & Exit Security L5 node; highest clinical urgency |

---

sections.title: Phase 2 — Company Enters
sections.content:
### 2.1 VN Anchor

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **Anchor type** | PROCESS\_VN |
| **Anchor unit** | Emergency Exit Door Alarm Guards |
| **Anchor level** | L5 |
| **Parent VN unit** | Facility Safety & Emergency Management (L6H-SAF) |
| **Parent level** | L6 |
| **Rationale** | Türwächter IS the standalone L5 system occupying the "IoT-enabled alarm guards with wireless monitoring" slot (15% share per VN). It is installed equipment used in running the CCRC — it never becomes part of the resident care outcome. The product also appears under L6d's Wander Prevention & Exit Security L5 node in the Memory Care context. |

---

### 2.2 Lens Formation

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **Function verb** | Protect (from L6H-SAF job\_family) |
| **Lens** | "What protection against unauthorized emergency exit use does a memory care facility need while maintaining instant fire-evacuation capability?" |
| **Segment filter A** | L6H-SAF is universal — all 5 segments pass Level A |
| **Segment filter B** | S1 (Memory Care) highest fit; S3 (SNF compliance) high fit; S2/S4 moderate; S5 portfolio context |

---

### 2.3 Core Job + Product Job

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **Job type** | Product Job (L5 anchor; parent = L6; scoped to the emergency exit security sub-system) |
| **Product Job Statement** | "Help Facility Safety & Emergency Management to secure all emergency exit doors against unauthorized use while maintaining fire-code-compliant single-motion evacuation capability and providing real-time door status visibility to operations staff — without mains power at each door point" |
| **Technology-agnostic test** | Holds for electromagnetic delayed-egress lock + wireless sensor, RFID proximity exit control + LoRaWAN gateway, or alarmed panic bar + BLE transmitter — **passes** |
| **3-alternative test** | RFID wander management / delayed-egress locks / coded keypads / manual patrol — **passes** |

---

### 2.4 Alternatives

Core Job (customer voice): *"Make sure no resident can slip out an emergency exit unnoticed, without us failing our next fire inspection or CMS survey."*

| Alternative | Category | Inherent trade-offs |
|---|---|---|
| **RFID/BLE Wander Management** (Stanley WanderGuard, CenTrak) | Security monitoring systems | Per-resident wristband required — fails if resident removes it; requires ceiling-mounted door antennas; cannot independently satisfy NFPA 101 single-motion escape requirements; $250K–$600K+ full facility deployment [[BEC Integrated]](https://becintegrated.com/wander-guard-system-cost-breakdown-and-pricing); 9,000+ facility installed base |
| **Electromagnetic Delayed-Egress Locks** (Assa Abloy, dormakaba) | Electromechanical locking devices | Power-dependent — must have battery backup for fire-safe operation; NFPA 101 §7.2.1.6 limits delay to max 30 s [[AHCA]](https://www.ahcancal.org/Survey-Regulatory-Legal/Documents/LSC%20Door%20Locking%20Provisions.21.pdf); licensed electrician required for installation and wiring; disruptive retrofit in existing buildings |
| **IoT Emergency Exit Alarm Guards** (GfS Türwächter) ← this product | Emergency exit door alarm guards (custom group) | Alarms but does not delay exit — cannot physically prevent elopement in the way a delayed-egress lock can; EasyWave protocol not native BACnet/KNX; ~€730 per door; pre-alarm false-trigger risk in high-traffic zones |
| **Coded Keypad Exits** | Access control terminals | No monitoring capability; NFPA 101 requires single-motion escape — keypad entry adds a step and may require AHJ approval; staff must memorize codes; codes expire or are shared |
| **Manual staff monitoring / do nothing** | — | Zero hardware cost; zero false alarms; inadequate overnight/weekend coverage; CMS F-tag immediate jeopardy exposure when elopement is documented; average elopement claim = $360,000 [[Tangram]](https://tangramins.com/news-insights/shocking-elopement-statistics-how-to-keep-wandering-residents-safe/) |

---

### 2.5 Scenarios and Output Type Activation

| Scenario | Output Types | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Sc1 — MC resident elopement attempt | MC | Primary use case; highest clinical and liability urgency |
| Sc2 — Staff/visitor props emergency exit open | IL, AL, MC, SNF, PAR | Universal across care levels |
| Sc3 — Fire emergency requiring instant evacuation | IL, AL, MC, SNF, PAR | Must not delay; guard must swing to open position with no additional steps |
| Sc4 — Annual CMS Life Safety Code survey | IL, AL, MC, SNF | Surveyor requests event logs and device status documentation |
| Sc5 — Battery depletion during low-activity overnight period | MC, SNF | Monitoring blind spot; staff away from remote exits; no alert on depletion |

**Sparsity check:** Sc1 activates MC only. Sc3–Sc4 activate multiple types. Core Job is correctly scoped. ✓

---

sections.title: Phase 3 — Needs Derivation
sections.content:
### 3.1 Burleson Pyramid

**Stakeholder roles:**

| Role | In this market | P-levels |
|---|---|---|
| Job Beneficiary | Memory care / dementia residents | P2, P5 |
| Job Executor | Facility safety staff, CNAs on unit floor | P1, P2, P3, P4, P5 |
| Job Overseer | Director of Nursing, Facility Administrator | P2, P3, P4 |
| Lifecycle Support | Maintenance Technician (install, battery, firmware) | P1: Acquisition, Preparation, Maintenance |
| Purchase Executor | CFO / VP Operations | P1: Acquisition |
| Purchase Influencer | CMS State Surveyor, Insurance Risk Manager | P2, P4 |

**P1 Lifecycle by Alternative (Job Executor — Memory Care Staff):**

| Stage | RFID Wander Mgmt | Delayed-Egress Lock | IoT Alarm Guard (Türwächter) | Manual Patrol |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Acquisition** | Enterprise RFP, RFID site survey, facility-wide cabling spec | Electrician quote, AHJ review for NFPA 101 compliance, power backup spec | Select guard + receiver + repeaters, specify key cylinder provisioning | Assign staff schedule; no procurement |
| **Preparation** | Mount door antennas, enroll residents in tag system, configure alert routing | Wire power to each door, install controller, program time delay and alarm | Mount guard on handle, provision keys, configure wireless receiver, set pre-alarm sensitivity | Brief staff on patrol schedule and manual log procedure |
| **Usage** | Monitor dashboard for tag proximity alerts; respond to events | Monitor for delay-activated alarms; respond within the 30-second window | Monitor dashboard for guard-state and alarm events; respond to activations; manage false alarms | Conduct physical door patrol every 30–60 min; log each check |
| **Maintenance** | Replace door antenna batteries; update resident tag enrollments; firmware updates | Test emergency release on schedule; replace battery backup annually; calibrate delay timer | Replace 9V battery per IoT module; confirm wireless transmission range after any building renovation | Revise patrol schedule for staffing changes |
| **Disposal** | Decommission server + software license; remove door antennas; dispose of tags | Deactivate and remove lock mechanism electrically; patch conduit | Remove guard from door handle; restore original handle hardware | End patrol program; zero hardware disposal |

**Key insight:** RFID wander management has the highest P1 Acquisition and Preparation burden. Delayed-egress locks have the highest Maintenance burden. The Türwächter's Acquisition and Preparation P1 burden is lowest after manual patrol — but its Usage burden includes active monitoring responsibility and false-alarm management.

---

### 3.2 P1 + P2 Needs — Job Map × Error Types

**Job Map for Product Job:**

| Step | Name | Relevant to product | Rationale |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | DEFINE | ✓ | Identify which doors require monitored coverage; specify alarm behavior by zone (MC vs. IL/AL/SNF) |
| 2 | LOCATE | — | Door locations are fixed building infrastructure |
| 3 | PREPARE | ✓ | Install, configure, commission |
| 4 | CONFIRM | ✓ | Verify armed state, battery, wireless link before each shift |
| 5 | EXECUTE | ✓ | Core function: secure handle, trigger alarm, transmit event |
| 6 | MONITOR | ✓ | Real-time event visibility across all doors across all shifts |
| 7 | MODIFY | ✓ | Alarm response, reset, proactive battery replacement |
| 8 | CONCLUDE | ✓ | Compliance logging, CMS Life Safety documentation |

**Relevant steps:** 7 of 8 (LOCATE excluded). Target: ≥2 needs per relevant step.

**Error Statements:**

| # | Step | Error Type | Statement | Pyramid | product\_related |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| N01 | DEFINE | ACCURACY | Minimize the likelihood of emergency exit doors in memory care perimeter zones being excluded from alarm guard coverage, resulting in uncovered exits identified during a CMS Life Safety Code survey | P2 | false |
| N02 | DEFINE | EFFORT | Increase the ability to determine complete alarm guard coverage requirements across a multi-level care campus in a single assessment without engaging a licensed engineer or fire protection consultant | P2 | false |
| N03 | PREPARE | EFFORT | Minimize the installation and commissioning time per door for emergency exit alarm monitoring systems across a 100-bed memory care unit without requiring mains electrical work at each door | P1 — Türwächter, Preparation | true |
| N04 | PREPARE | ACCURACY | Minimize the likelihood of incorrect pre-alarm sensitivity configuration causing false alarm activations exceeding 2 incidents per door per month during normal staff passage in a memory care corridor | P1 — Türwächter, Preparation | true |
| N05 | CONFIRM | DETECTION | Increase the ability to detect a de-armed or non-functional emergency exit alarm guard before the condition is identified during a CMS inspection, with detection lag under 4 hours | P2 | false |
| N06 | CONFIRM | ACCURACY | Minimize the likelihood of a door alarm guard displaying "armed" status in the monitoring dashboard while the mechanical guard is physically disabled or in the open position | P1 — Türwächter, Usage | true |
| N07 | EXECUTE | ROBUSTNESS | Minimize failures of emergency exit alarm activation during facility-wide power outage conditions lasting more than 8 hours | P2 | false |
| N08 | EXECUTE | ACCURACY | Minimize the likelihood of the exit alarm triggering due to environmental vibration or thermal cycling rather than intentional guard actuation in memory care zones where false alarms prompt immediate staff emergency response | P2 | false |
| N09 | EXECUTE | THROUGHPUT | Increase the ability to deploy monitored alarm coverage across all emergency exits in a 200-bed CCRC campus from a single centralized receiver without requiring per-door electrical infrastructure | P1 — Türwächter, Preparation | true |
| N10 | MONITOR | DETECTION | Minimize the time from unauthorized emergency exit door activation to staff notification across all care levels and shifts, including overnight and weekend periods | P2 | false |
| N11 | MONITOR | CONSISTENCY | Minimize variability in alarm event delivery reliability across structurally diverse building zones including basement corridors, reinforced concrete wings, and exterior stairwells | P1 — Türwächter, Usage | true |
| N12 | MONITOR | THROUGHPUT | Increase the ability to view live armed/unarmed status of all emergency exits across all care levels from a single monitoring interface without logging into multiple vendor platforms | P2 | false |
| N13 | MONITOR | DETECTION | Minimize the number of IoT module battery depletion events per year that go undetected until monitoring coverage is lost on an active exit door | P1 — Türwächter, Maintenance | true |
| N14 | MODIFY | RECOVERY | Minimize the total time to restore alarm guard coverage at a door following a battery replacement event, from detection of depletion to confirmed wireless re-connection | P1 — Türwächter, Maintenance | true |
| N15 | MODIFY | EFFORT | Increase the ability of a single night-shift CNA to manage simultaneous door alarm events across a 60-bed memory care floor while maintaining resident monitoring duties without requiring supervisor escalation for each event | P2 | false |
| N16 | MODIFY | ROBUSTNESS | Minimize failures in alarm guard reset capability following a valid elopement activation where the triggering resident may have applied force to the guard mechanism during the exit attempt | P2 | false |
| N17 | CONCLUDE | EFFORT | Increase the ability to produce audit-ready CMS Life Safety Code compliance documentation covering all emergency exit events without manual log consolidation by facility staff | P2 | false |
| N18 | CONCLUDE | ACCURACY | Minimize the likelihood of incomplete or missing emergency exit event records at annual CMS survey time due to gaps in manual event logging across shifts | P2 | false |
| N19 | CONCLUDE | CONSISTENCY | Minimize variability in emergency exit event record completeness across different shifts and staff members, ensuring documentation meets CMS survey standards regardless of which staff member was on duty | P2 | false |

---

### 3.3 P3–P5 Bridge Questions

| # | Level | Role | Retrieval | Bridge | Market Need | Flag |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| N20 | P3 Role | Director of Nursing / Administrator | "Who does this person become by managing exit security well?" → The administrator with zero elopement incidents and zero Life Safety citations on record | "What must the system do so they consistently hold that record?" | Maximize the ability to document zero unmonitored emergency exit events per quarter as evidence of proactive Life Safety compliance posture to CMS, families, and insurers | — |
| N21 | P4 Image | Director of Nursing / Administrator | "Who is watching? What must they think?" → CMS State Surveyors, resident families, insurance underwriters | "What must the product do so those observers see a competent, prepared operator?" | Minimize the risk of a CMS Immediate Jeopardy citation arising from a documented elopement through an exit door that lacked active monitoring at the time of the incident | — |
| N22 | P5 Emotional | Memory Care Floor Staff (CNAs / charge nurses) | "What do they want to feel?" → Immediate, reliable awareness; "Avoid?" → Being the on-duty staff member when a resident got out undetected | "What must the product do so they feel reliably covered, not anxious?" | Minimize the likelihood of on-duty memory care staff remaining unaware of a resident's emergency exit activation for more than 30 seconds after the event | [hypothesis] |

---

### 3.4 ODI Scoring

| # | Summary | Imp. | Sat. | Opp. | Class | Impact | Conf. |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| N01 | Uncovered MC exits at CMS survey | 9 | 5 | 13 | **Underserved** | safety | 0.90 |
| N02 | Assessment time without engineer | 5 | 6 | 5 | Adequate | time | 0.70 |
| N03 | Installation time per door, no mains | 7 | 5 | 9 | **Underserved** | time | 0.85 |
| N04 | Pre-alarm false trigger rate | 7 | 5 | 9 | **Underserved** | reliability | 0.80 |
| N05 | Non-functional guard detection lag | 9 | 3 | 15 | **Underserved** | safety | 0.85 |
| N06 | Armed/armed status accuracy | 8 | 4 | 12 | **Underserved** | safety | 0.80 |
| N07 | Alarm reliability at 0V / power outage | 10 | 6 | 14 | **Underserved** | safety | 0.92 |
| N08 | Environmental false alarm rate | 7 | 5 | 9 | **Underserved** | reliability | 0.80 |
| N09 | Multi-door coverage, single receiver | 6 | 5 | 7 | Adequate | cost | 0.75 |
| N10 | Alert latency overnight/weekend | 9 | 4 | 14 | **Underserved** | safety | 0.88 |
| N11 | Signal reliability across building zones | 7 | 4 | 10 | **Underserved** | reliability | 0.80 |
| N12 | Single dashboard across care levels | 6 | 3 | 9 | **Underserved** | time | 0.75 |
| N13 | Undetected battery depletion | 8 | 3 | 13 | **Underserved** | safety | 0.85 |
| N14 | Battery replacement recovery time | 6 | 5 | 7 | Adequate | time | 0.75 |
| N15 | Night-shift multi-alarm cognitive burden | 7 | 4 | 10 | **Underserved** | skill | 0.70 |
| N16 | Guard reset after physical damage | 6 | 5 | 7 | Adequate | reliability | 0.65 |
| N17 | CMS documentation generation | 8 | 3 | 13 | **Underserved** | time | 0.85 |
| N18 | Documentation completeness at survey | 9 | 3 | 15 | **Underserved** | safety | 0.88 |
| N19 | Cross-shift log consistency | 7 | 3 | 11 | **Underserved** | reliability | 0.80 |
| N20 | Documented zero-elopement-through-monitored record | 8 | 4 | 12 | **Underserved** | safety | 0.80 |
| N21 | CMS Immediate Jeopardy citation risk | 10 | 5 | 15 | **Underserved** | safety | 0.90 |
| N22 | Staff awareness lag in MC | 8 | 4 | 12 | **Underserved** | safety | 0.75 |

**Opportunity Score formula:** importance + max(importance − satisfaction, 0)
**Underserved threshold:** importance > 7 AND satisfaction < 5

**Top 5 opportunities by score:**
1. N05 / N18 / N21 — score 15 (non-functional guard detection, documentation completeness, CMS IJ risk)
2. N07 / N10 — score 14 (power-fail alarm reliability, alert latency)
3. N01 / N13 / N17 — score 13 (uncovered exits, undetected battery depletion, CMS documentation effort)

---

**Yield check:**

| Metric | Target | Actual |
|---|---|---|
| Total error statements | 15–30 | **22** ✓ |
| Needs per relevant job step | ≥2 | Min 2 per step ✓ |
| Market-inherent (product\_related=false) | 60–70% | N01, N02, N05, N07, N08, N10, N12, N15, N16, N17, N18, N19, N20, N21, N22 = **68%** ✓ |
| Technology-specific (product\_related=true) | 30–40% | N03, N04, N06, N09, N11, N13, N14 = **32%** ✓ |
| Underserved needs (opp ≥13) | — | **9 needs** at opportunity score ≥13 |
| "Minimize" / "Increase" mix | ~60/40 | 15 Minimize / 7 Increase = **68/32** (acceptable) ✓ |

---

sections.title: Sources
sections.content:
- [Shocking Elopement Statistics — Tangram Insurance](https://tangramins.com/news-insights/shocking-elopement-statistics-how-to-keep-wandering-residents-safe/)
- [CMS Life Safety Code Requirements](https://www.cms.gov/medicare/health-safety-standards/certification-compliance/life-safety-code-health-care-facilities-code-requirements)
- [AHCA Door Locking Provisions (NFPA 101 § delayed egress)](https://www.ahcancal.org/Survey-Regulatory-Legal/Documents/LSC%20Door%20Locking%20Provisions.21.pdf)
- [Decoded: Deterring Elopement in Health Care Facilities — I Dig Hardware](https://idighardware.com/2018/07/decoded-deterring-egress-in-health-care-facilities/)
- [WanderGuard System Cost Breakdown — BEC Integrated](https://becintegrated.com/wander-guard-system-cost-breakdown-and-pricing)
- [WanderGuard BLUE — Securitas Healthcare](https://www.securitashealthcare.com/solutions/wander-management)
- [ECRI: Preventing Harm — Emerging Technologies for Wandering and Elopement](https://home.ecri.org/blogs/ecri-blog/preventing-harm-in-aging-services-emerging-technologies-to-mitigate-hazardous-wandering-and-elopement)
- [Wikipedia: Continuing Care Retirement Communities in the United States](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Continuing_care_retirement_communities_in_the_United_States)

rows.CFJ: Provide safe, integrated residential accommodation and healthcare services across a continuum of care levels to older adults, enabling aging in place with progressively intensive clinical support as functional needs evolve
rows.Output Types: Independent Living (IL), Assisted Living (AL), Memory Care (MC), Skilled Nursing (SNF), Post-Acute Rehab (PAR)

rows.Circumstance: Residents with dementia/cognitive impairment; 60% at elopement risk; ~1–2% elope annually; 32–40% of elopements fatal within 24 h [[Tangram]](https://tangramins.com/news-insights/shocking-elopement-statistics-how-to-keep-wandering-residents-safe/)
rows.Why distinct: Alternatives differ: wander management (RFID tags) dominates; exits must be secured against resident-initiated actuation while remaining fire-code compliant

rows.Circumstance: Functionally capable residents; no resident-initiated elopement risk; standard fire exit compliance
rows.Why distinct: Simpler alternatives hired: basic mechanical alarms, wired panels; lower urgency

rows.Circumstance: 24-h clinical care; highest CMS regulatory scrutiny; annual Life Safety survey; Immediate Jeopardy penalties >$10,000/day for elopement gaps [[CMS]](https://www.cms.gov/medicare/health-safety-standards/certification-compliance/life-safety-code-health-care-facilities-code-requirements)
rows.Why distinct: Compliance documentation and audit-trail quality are the primary buying criteria

rows.Circumstance: Short-stay, high resident throughput; fire safety primary; low elopement risk
rows.Why distinct: Simpler exit monitoring acceptable; false-alarm tolerance higher than MC

rows.Circumstance: Manages many campuses; procurement is portfolio-level; needs centralized dashboard across properties
rows.Why distinct: Different alternatives hired: enterprise wander-management platforms vs. per-door retrofit devices

rows.Job family: Protect residents, staff, and assets from safety incidents at all times
rows.Türwächter relevance: **Primary anchor** — Emergency Exit Door Alarm Guards sits at L5 under this node

rows.Job family: Provide secured residential care for residents with dementia and cognitive impairment
rows.Türwächter relevance: **Secondary context** — Wander Prevention & Exit Security L5 node; highest clinical urgency

rows.Value: Türwächter IS the standalone L5 system occupying the "IoT-enabled alarm guards with wireless monitoring" slot (15% share per VN). It is installed equipment used in running the CCRC — it never becomes part of the resident care outcome. The product also appears under L6d's Wander Prevention & Exit Security L5 node in the Memory Care context.
"What protection against unauthorized emergency exit use does a memory care facility need while maintaining instant fire-evacuation capability?"
L6H-SAF is universal — all 5 segments pass Level A
S1 (Memory Care) highest fit; S3 (SNF compliance) high fit; S2/S4 moderate; S5 portfolio context
Product Job (L5 anchor; parent = L6; scoped to the emergency exit security sub-system)

"Help Facility Safety & Emergency Management to secure all emergency exit doors against unauthorized use while maintaining fire-code-compliant single-motion evacuation capability and providing real-time door status visibility to operations staff — without mains power at each door point"
Holds for electromagnetic delayed-egress lock + wireless sensor, RFID proximity exit control + LoRaWAN gateway, or alarmed panic bar + BLE transmitter — **passes**
RFID wander management / delayed-egress locks / coded keypads / manual patrol — **passes**

rows.Inherent trade-offs: Per-resident wristband required — fails if resident removes it; requires ceiling-mounted door antennas; cannot independently satisfy NFPA 101 single-motion escape requirements; $250K–$600K+ full facility deployment [[BEC Integrated]](https://becintegrated.com/wander-guard-system-cost-breakdown-and-pricing); 9,000+ facility installed base

Power-dependent — must have battery backup for fire-safe operation; NFPA 101 §7.2.1.6 limits delay to max 30 s [[AHCA]](https://www.ahcancal.org/Survey-Regulatory-Legal/Documents/LSC%20Door%20Locking%20Provisions.21.pdf); licensed electrician required for installation and wiring; disruptive retrofit in existing buildings

rows.Alternative: **IoT Emergency Exit Alarm Guards** (GfS Türwächter) ← this product
rows.Category: Emergency exit door alarm guards (custom group)
rows.Inherent trade-offs: Alarms but does not delay exit — cannot physically prevent elopement in the way a delayed-egress lock can; EasyWave protocol not native BACnet/KNX; ~€730 per door; pre-alarm false-trigger risk in high-traffic zones
No monitoring capability; NFPA 101 requires single-motion escape — keypad entry adds a step and may require AHJ approval; staff must memorize codes; codes expire or are shared

Zero hardware cost; zero false alarms; inadequate overnight/weekend coverage; CMS F-tag immediate jeopardy exposure when elopement is documented; average elopement claim = $360,000 [[Tangram]](https://tangramins.com/news-insights/shocking-elopement-statistics-how-to-keep-wandering-residents-safe/)

rows.Notes: Primary use case; highest clinical and liability urgency

rows.Scenario: Sc2 — Staff/visitor props emergency exit open
Sc3 — Fire emergency requiring instant evacuation
rows.Notes: Must not delay; guard must swing to open position with no additional steps

rows.Scenario: Sc4 — Annual CMS Life Safety Code survey
rows.Notes: Surveyor requests event logs and device status documentation

rows.Scenario: Sc5 — Battery depletion during low-activity overnight period
rows.Notes: Monitoring blind spot; staff away from remote exits; no alert on depletion

rows.In this market: Facility safety staff, CNAs on unit floor
CMS State Surveyor, Insurance Risk Manager

rows.RFID Wander Mgmt: Enterprise RFP, RFID site survey, facility-wide cabling spec
rows.Delayed-Egress Lock: Electrician quote, AHJ review for NFPA 101 compliance, power backup spec
rows.IoT Alarm Guard (Türwächter): Select guard + receiver + repeaters, specify key cylinder provisioning

rows.RFID Wander Mgmt: Mount door antennas, enroll residents in tag system, configure alert routing
rows.Delayed-Egress Lock: Wire power to each door, install controller, program time delay and alarm
rows.IoT Alarm Guard (Türwächter): Mount guard on handle, provision keys, configure wireless receiver, set pre-alarm sensitivity
rows.Manual Patrol: Brief staff on patrol schedule and manual log procedure

rows.RFID Wander Mgmt: Monitor dashboard for tag proximity alerts; respond to events
rows.Delayed-Egress Lock: Monitor for delay-activated alarms; respond within the 30-second window
rows.IoT Alarm Guard (Türwächter): Monitor dashboard for guard-state and alarm events; respond to activations; manage false alarms
rows.Manual Patrol: Conduct physical door patrol every 30–60 min; log each check

rows.RFID Wander Mgmt: Replace door antenna batteries; update resident tag enrollments; firmware updates
rows.Delayed-Egress Lock: Test emergency release on schedule; replace battery backup annually; calibrate delay timer
rows.IoT Alarm Guard (Türwächter): Replace 9V battery per IoT module; confirm wireless transmission range after any building renovation
rows.Manual Patrol: Revise patrol schedule for staffing changes

rows.RFID Wander Mgmt: Decommission server + software license; remove door antennas; dispose of tags
rows.Delayed-Egress Lock: Deactivate and remove lock mechanism electrically; patch conduit
rows.IoT Alarm Guard (Türwächter): Remove guard from door handle; restore original handle hardware
rows.Manual Patrol: End patrol program; zero hardware disposal

rows.Name: DEFINE
rows.Rationale: Identify which doors require monitored coverage; specify alarm behavior by zone (MC vs. IL/AL/SNF)

rows.Name: LOCATE
rows.Rationale: Door locations are fixed building infrastructure

rows.Name: CONFIRM
rows.Rationale: Verify armed state, battery, wireless link before each shift

rows.Name: EXECUTE
rows.Rationale: Core function: secure handle, trigger alarm, transmit event

rows.Name: MONITOR
rows.Rationale: Real-time event visibility across all doors across all shifts

rows.Name: MODIFY
rows.Rationale: Alarm response, reset, proactive battery replacement

rows.Name: CONCLUDE
rows.Rationale: Compliance logging, CMS Life Safety documentation

rows.Statement: Minimize the likelihood of emergency exit doors in memory care perimeter zones being excluded from alarm guard coverage, resulting in uncovered exits identified during a CMS Life Safety Code survey
Increase the ability to determine complete alarm guard coverage requirements across a multi-level care campus in a single assessment without engaging a licensed engineer or fire protection consultant
Minimize the installation and commissioning time per door for emergency exit alarm monitoring systems across a 100-bed memory care unit without requiring mains electrical work at each door
Minimize the likelihood of incorrect pre-alarm sensitivity configuration causing false alarm activations exceeding 2 incidents per door per month during normal staff passage in a memory care corridor
Increase the ability to detect a de-armed or non-functional emergency exit alarm guard before the condition is identified during a CMS inspection, with detection lag under 4 hours
Minimize the likelihood of a door alarm guard displaying "armed" status in the monitoring dashboard while the mechanical guard is physically disabled or in the open position
Minimize failures of emergency exit alarm activation during facility-wide power outage conditions lasting more than 8 hours

Minimize the likelihood of the exit alarm triggering due to environmental vibration or thermal cycling rather than intentional guard actuation in memory care zones where false alarms prompt immediate staff emergency response
Increase the ability to deploy monitored alarm coverage across all emergency exits in a 200-bed CCRC campus from a single centralized receiver without requiring per-door electrical infrastructure
Minimize the time from unauthorized emergency exit door activation to staff notification across all care levels and shifts, including overnight and weekend periods
Minimize variability in alarm event delivery reliability across structurally diverse building zones including basement corridors, reinforced concrete wings, and exterior stairwells
Increase the ability to view live armed/unarmed status of all emergency exits across all care levels from a single monitoring interface without logging into multiple vendor platforms
Minimize the number of IoT module battery depletion events per year that go undetected until monitoring coverage is lost on an active exit door
Minimize the total time to restore alarm guard coverage at a door following a battery replacement event, from detection of depletion to confirmed wireless re-connection

Increase the ability of a single night-shift CNA to manage simultaneous door alarm events across a 60-bed memory care floor while maintaining resident monitoring duties without requiring supervisor escalation for each event
Minimize failures in alarm guard reset capability following a valid elopement activation where the triggering resident may have applied force to the guard mechanism during the exit attempt
Increase the ability to produce audit-ready CMS Life Safety Code compliance documentation covering all emergency exit events without manual log consolidation by facility staff
Minimize the likelihood of incomplete or missing emergency exit event records at annual CMS survey time due to gaps in manual event logging across shifts

Minimize variability in emergency exit event record completeness across different shifts and staff members, ensuring documentation meets CMS survey standards regardless of which staff member was on duty

rows.Retrieval: "Who does this person become by managing exit security well?" → The administrator with zero elopement incidents and zero Life Safety citations on record
rows.Bridge: "What must the system do so they consistently hold that record?"
rows.Market Need: Maximize the ability to document zero unmonitored emergency exit events per quarter as evidence of proactive Life Safety compliance posture to CMS, families, and insurers

rows.Retrieval: "Who is watching? What must they think?" → CMS State Surveyors, resident families, insurance underwriters
rows.Bridge: "What must the product do so those observers see a competent, prepared operator?"
rows.Market Need: Minimize the risk of a CMS Immediate Jeopardy citation arising from a documented elopement through an exit door that lacked active monitoring at the time of the incident

rows.Role: Memory Care Floor Staff (CNAs / charge nurses)
rows.Retrieval: "What do they want to feel?" → Immediate, reliable awareness; "Avoid?" → Being the on-duty staff member when a resident got out undetected
rows.Bridge: "What must the product do so they feel reliably covered, not anxious?"
rows.Market Need: Minimize the likelihood of on-duty memory care staff remaining unaware of a resident's emergency exit activation for more than 30 seconds after the event

rows.Summary: Uncovered MC exits at CMS survey
Installation time per door, no mains

job_map.product_job_statement: Help Facility Safety & Emergency Management to secure all emergency exit doors against unauthorized use while maintaining fire-code-compliant single-motion evacuation capability and providing real-time door status visibility to operations staff — without mains power at each door point
job_map.cfj: Provide safe, integrated residential accommodation and healthcare services across a continuum of care levels to older adults, enabling aging in place with progressively intensive clinical support as functional needs evolve

job_steps.relevance_rationale: Identify which exits require monitored coverage; specify alarm behavior per zone (MC vs IL/AL/SNF)
Door locations are fixed building infrastructure
Install, configure, commission alarm guards and receivers
Verify armed state, battery charge, wireless link before each shift
Core function: secure handle, trigger alarm, transmit event to monitoring system
Real-time door status and alarm event visibility across all exits and all shifts
Alarm response, guard reset after activation, proactive battery replacement
Compliance event logging, CMS Life Safety Code documentation, inspection archive

incumbent_anchors.rationale: Largest share at 40% in memory care units; per-resident wristband approach; requires ceiling-mounted door antennas; does not independently satisfy NFPA 101 escape requirements
30% share; NFPA 101 §7.2.1.6 compliant for healthcare; power-dependent with battery backup requirement; licensed electrician for installation
15% share; no monitoring capability; potential NFPA 101 single-motion compliance issue without automatic release integration
Status quo / do nothing; zero acquisition cost; inadequate overnight coverage; CMS immediate jeopardy exposure; average elopement claim $360,000
